coredata.dc:title: TiO2/Graphene Quantum Dots core-shell based photo anodes with TTIP treatment- A perspective way of enhancing the short circuit current
coredata.prism:publicationName: Solar Energy Materials and Solar Cells
coredata.dc:creator: Peter, I. John
Rajamanickam, N.
Vijaya, S.
Anandan, S.
Ramachandran, K.
Nithiananthi, P.
coredata.dc:description: Solar Cell with TiO2-Graphene Quantum core/shell heterostructure synthesized by surface functionalization route is used for the present study. The structural properties of Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) coated TiO2 are confirmed by XRD, Raman, SEM, TEM and mapping analysis. Energies of the Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO) and Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO) of the GQDs against vacuum are determined using cyclic voltammetry technique and ultraviolet photo electron spectroscopy. When compared to the bare devices, J-V characteristics of the GQDs incorporated solar cell show an enhancement in efficiency around 38% and Jsc of 48%. Moreover, TiO2-GQD (TG 10) device pre and post treated with Titanium Tetra Isopropoxide (TTIP) shows the highest power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 5.1% which is ~65% enhancement when compared to untreated TG 10 based cell. This enhancement is due to higher light harvesting capacity, reduction in charge transfer resistance (Rct) at the interface, increase in life time of carriers and increase in charge transfer rates.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Solar cell
Core-shell
Graphene quantum dots
Surface functionalization
TTIP treatment

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Solar Energy Materials and Solar Cells

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of HOMO and LUMO energy levels

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Formation mechanism of core shell nanostructure.
(a) XRD spectra of bare TiO2 and TiO2-GQD core shell nanostructures (b) Variation of FWHM for different GQD concentrations.
(a) UV–Visible spectrum of pure and GQDs modified TiO2 (b) Tauc's plot (c–d) Excitation dependent photoluminescence spectrum of prepared GQDs.
Raman spectra of (a) bare TiO2 (b) GQDs modified TiO2 (TG 10) core shell nanostructures.
Pristine TiO2 (a) SEM (b–d) Mapping and EDAX spectrum respectively. TG 10 (e) SEM (f–j) Mapping and EDAX spectrum respectively.

TEM micrograph of (a–b) pristine TiO2 (d) SEM image of the Prepared GO. TEM micrograph of (e) amino-functionalized GQDs (g–j) HRTEM image of TiO2-GQDs (TG 10) core shell nanostructures (k) Mapping spectrum of TG 10 (c, f and l) average particle size distribution spectrum of pristine TiO2, GQDs and TG 10 respectively.

(a) Cyclic voltammogram of GQDs deposited on graphite sheet electrode (b) UPS spectrum of GQDs (c) Tauc's plot for determining the band gap of GQDs (optical absorption spectrum of GQDs is plotted at the inset) (d) Schematic illustration of charge transfer process in TiO2-GQDs core shell nanostructure.

(a–c) J-V, Nyquist and Bode plot for pristine and TiO2-GQDs core shell nanostructures respectively (d–f) TTIP treated J-V, Nyquist and Bode plot for pristine and TiO2-GQDs core shell nanostructures respectively.
Measured power for (a) without and (b) with TTIP treated solar cells, (c–d) variation of Jsc and PCE (η) for untreated and TTIP treated cells (e–f) error bar plot for TTIP treated cells.
Measured photovoltaic parameters for pristine and TiO2-GQDs core shell nanostructure based DSSC.
Useful parameters from EIS of different photoanodes.
Photovoltaic parameters of DSSC in the present and previous reported works.

head.ce:title: TiO2/Graphene Quantum Dots core-shell based photo anodes with TTIP treatment- A perspective way of enhancing the short circuit current

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanostructure Lab, Department of Physics, The Gandhigram Rural Institute – Deemed to be University, Gandhigram, 624302, Tamilnadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Gandhigram Rural Institute – Deemed to be University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanostructure Lab, Department of Physics, The Gandhigram Rural Institute - Deemed to be University, Gandhigram, 624302, Tamilnadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanomaterials & Solar Energy Conversion Lab, Department of Chemistry, National Institute of Technology, Trichy, 620015, Tamilnadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanomaterials & Solar Energy Conversion Lab, Department of Chemistry, National Institute of Technology, Trichy, 620015, Tamilnadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Solar Cell with TiO2-Graphene Quantum core/shell heterostructure synthesized by surface functionalization route is used for the present study. The structural properties of Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) coated TiO2 are confirmed by XRD, Raman, SEM, TEM and mapping analysis. Energies of the Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO) and Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO) of the GQDs against vacuum are determined using cyclic voltammetry technique and ultraviolet photo electron spectroscopy. When compared to the bare devices, J-V characteristics of the GQDs incorporated solar cell show an enhancement in efficiency around 38% and Jsc of 48%. Moreover, TiO2-GQD (TG 10) device pre and post treated with Titanium Tetra Isopropoxide (TTIP) shows the highest power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 5.1% which is ~65% enhancement when compared to untreated TG 10 based cell. This enhancement is due to higher light harvesting capacity, reduction in charge transfer resistance (Rct) at the interface, increase in life time of carriers and increase in charge transfer rates.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dot solar cell (QDSC) is one of the most important derivative of the conventional dye sensitized solar cells (DSSC), wherein quantum dots (QDs) are employed as photon absorber or sensitizer. It replaces the conventional organic sensitizer because of the excellent properties like tunable energy bandgap with size, large optical absorption coefficient, large dipole moment for enhanced charge separation and multiple exciton generation (MEG) [1–3]. Nevertheless, even today after stages of development the power conversion efficiency (PCE) remains inadequate for practical applications. The most important tool to achieve a better PCE is the sufficient usage of solar spectrum. The other important approach is co-sensitization, which involves the combination of two quantum dots with different bandgaps for the expansion of the light absorption range (both visible and NIR regions) leading to increase of light harvesting capacity. This may be done by combining quantum dot structures such as core-shell or doping and alloying to form binary or ternary quantum dots (QDs). The QDs can be made from different materials like CdTe, CdSe, CdS, PbS, Ag2S, Bi2S3, In2S3, Sb2S3, and Sb2Se3 where their bandgaps can be easily tuned by optimizing the particle size [4–18]. Christian Herzog et al. have reported that when combining two semiconductors the recombination probability of photo generated charge carriers are reduced due to local charge separation and diffusion length [19]. Another convenient way is incorporation of highly conducting carbon nanostructures, (carbon nanotubes or reduced graphene oxide) which can boost the current collecting capacity [20–22]. Among the carbon materials, graphene-based materials can be effectively used due to its remarkable properties. Further, tuning the band gap of zero-dimensional Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) would provide unique electron transport properties and new phenomenon arising due to quantum confinement. As early as in 2012, Mrinal Dutta et al. [23] have synthesized GQDs by a direct chemical method in combination with ZnO nanowires (NWs) for photovoltaic cells and found an open circuit voltage of 0.8 V. Recently, Yuanyuan Zhong et al. [7] have reported an improved PCE of 6.59% in GQD on the CdSe film. Here we report the photovoltaic performance of TiO2-GQDs core shell nanostructures based solar cells as such study is not reported. Also, the electrochemical behaviors are studied through Nyquist and bode phase plots, which are essential for the complete understanding of the fabricated solar cells for stability and improved performance in terms of the PCE.

For better conversion efficiency, sufficient surface area for dye adsorption is very important. So, without sacrificing the efficient electron transport, most of the researchers tried branched nanostructures to increase the surface area. But in all cases, the interfacial recombination rate is one of the major limiting parameter for DSSC efficiency. Also the slow electron transport rate resulting from multiple trapping/detrapping events occurring within grain boundaries would lead to high interface recombination and limit the device efficiency. Using core/shell structures is an effective tool to compromise this effect and to enhance the conversion efficiency. Core/shell nanostructures lower the charge recombination by forming a coating layer which builds up an energy barrier at the semiconductor/electrolyte interface and thus retards the reaction between the photogenerated electrons and the redox species in electrolyte [24]. In general core/shell nanoporous electrode consists of a Metal Oxide Semiconductor (MOS) matrix that is covered with a shell of other metal-oxide. In this work, we have used GQD.

Graphite powder (325 mesh) from Alfa Aesar, 98% sulfuric acid (H2SO4), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), 30% hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), Ammonia solution, Titanium (IV) Isopropoxide (Ti[OCH(CH3)2]4), Isopropyl alcohol ((CH3)2CHOH), Ethanol, Acetyl acetone (99%), Triton-X 100 (95%), from Merck were used in the present work.

TiO2 nanoparticles were prepared by the method of hydrolysis as reported by John Peter et al. [25] where titanium (IV) isopropoxide Ti[OCH(CH3)2]4) was used as a precursor. Initially, 5 ml of Ti[OCH(CH3)2]4 was dissolved in 100 ml of isopropyl alcohol ((CH3)2CHOH) and the solution was sonicated for 10 min and stirred continuously for 1 h at room temperature. After stirring, 400 ml of deionized water was quickly added to the above mixture and the stirring is continued for 30 more minutes. The resultant solution was aged for 3 h, then centrifuged, washed with deionized water and ethanol and dried at 60 °C for whole night. Then the prepared TiO2nanoparticle was calcinated at 450 °C for 2 h in air.

Graphene oxide (GO) sheets were prepared from natural graphite by using a modified Hummer's method [26]. The GQDs were prepared under hydrothermal condition with H2O2 as an etching agent and ammonia as an assistant. In a typical preparation, 5 mg of graphene sheet (GS) was dissolved in 30 ml of deionized water followed by adding ammonia solution (to maintain a pH = 10) and H2O2 (2 mL). This mixture was stirred for 1 h, following which ultra-sonication was carried out for another 30 min. The resultant solution was transferred to (polytetrafluoroethylene) Teflon-lined autoclave (80 mL) and heated at 180 °C for 5 h by hydrothermal treatment. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting black suspension was washed using deionized water and ethanol (Solution-A). Finally, the colloidal paste was used for further characterizations. The same procedure was followed for all concentrations of GQDs (10 mg, 20 mg, 30 mg).

Typically, 5 ml of Ti[OCH(CH3)2]4 was dissolved in 100 ml of isopropyl alcohol ((CH3)2CHOH) and the resultant solution was sonicated for 10 min and stirred continuously for 1 h at room temperature. After stirring, 400 ml of deionized water was quickly added to the above mixture and stirred for 30 min (Solution-B). Then the solution-A was added slowly into solution-B, while continually stirring. Subsequently, the mixed solution was heated to 95 °C and maintained at this temperature for 2 h. The colour of the TiO2–GQDs mixture then changed to greyish-white. This was subjected to repeated washing with ethanol by centrifugation and finally with water. The final TiO2–GQDs powder was obtained after drying the product at 60°Cand this is denoted as TG-5. Likewise TG-10, TG-20, TG-30 stand for 10 mg GQDs, 20 mg GQDs, 30 mg GQDs respectively.

Surface functionalization route was used to synthesize TiO2-GQDs core shell nano-architecture with little modification from the method reported in literature [27]. Initially the prepared TiO2 surface should be activated to its surrounding temperature. Then the prepared colloidal GQD was transferred to the above solution in drops at the same temperature. On such addition, GQDs would be attracted towards the TiO2 surface due to its electrostatic attraction and the solution colour changed from white to greyish-white. This shows that GQDs were coated on the surface of TiO2. Thus core shell structure is formed, whose mechanism is symbolically represented in Fig. 1 .

The structural properties of TiO2 and TiO2-GQDs core shell nanostructure are characterized by X-ray diffraction with an X'Pert Pro-PAnalytic diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å). The quality and presence of GQDs on TiO2 are characterized by Raman spectroscopy (PANalytical X'Pert Raman spectrometer) excited at 532 nm. The morphology of the sample was characterized by Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) (TESCAN VEGA-3 LMU) and Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) (Jeol/JEM 2100). UV–Visible absorption spectra were obtained on PerkinElmer UV spectrophotometer at room temperature in the wavelength from 200 to 800 nm. Photoluminescence characterization was done using luminescence spectrometer with xenon lamp as a source of excitation. Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) was done by ESCALAB Xi + spectrometer. Electrochemical analyzer CHI 660 D was used to record the cyclic voltammetry in an aqueous 0.5 M of Na2SO4 electrolyte with a three electrode cell, where GQDs, platinum wire and Ag/AgCl were used as the working electrode, counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. The photovoltaic characterizations of the fabricated DSSCs were analyzed under illumination of 150 W Xe light source from a solar simulator in combination with standard AM1.5 (85 mW cm−2). Electrochemical and Photo-electrochemical measurements were conducted through an AUTOLAB12/FRA2 electrochemical analyzer.

Pristine and TiO2/GQDs films were prepared on Fluorine doped Tin Oxide (FTO) substrates via doctor-blade method as reported by Rajamanickam et al. [28]. FTO glass plates were used as substrates for the preparation of photoanodes and counter electrodes. Before coating the anodes, FTO plates were washed by sonicating in a soap solution, distilled water and 2-propanol for 15 min. (i) Without titanium isopropoxide(TTIP)treatment
: Initially, the synthesized TiO2 and TiO2-GQD powders were made into fine paste using distilled water (0.34 ml), acetyl acetone (0.02 ml) and triton-X (20 μl). Then the resultant paste was coated on FTO substrate by using doctor blade technique with a cell area of 0.25 cm2. The coated films were annealed at 450 °C for 30 min in air atmosphere. (ii)Treatment with TTIP:
The procedure was similar except that before and after coating the synthesized nanoparticles, the TiO2 blocking layer was coated by 0.2 M titanium (IV) isopropoxide solution by spin-coating (2000 rpm, 60 s).

The prepared films were sensitized by dipping in a 3 × 10−4 M solution of N719 dye in ethanol for 24 h. After removing from the solution, the dye adsorbed TiO2 films were rinsed using ethanol to remove excess adsorbed products. The Pt coated counter electrode for the DSSC was prepared by drop casting of 5 × 10−4 M solution of chloroplatinic acid in isopropanol on FTO plate followed by sintering at 450 °C for 30 min in air atmosphere. Iodolyte (iodide/tri-iodide) was used as the redox electrolyte.

Solar cell is fabricated with this photoanode following the usual procedure and hence is not repeated here. The electrolyte was injected between the anode and counter electrode by firmly pressing them. A thin layer of parafilm was used as a spacer to avoid short circuit. A binder clip was fixed externally to sustain the mechanical grip without any further sealing, which completed the assembly of the DSSC.

XRD spectra of pristine and TiO2-GQDs core shell nanostructure combinations are presented in Fig. 2 a. The XRD pattern of the uncoated TiO2 nanoparticles is of tetragonal structure and the peaks are well assigned to anatase phase (JCPDS card 04–0477). In TiO2-GQD core shell nanostructures, the presence of graphene on the TiO2 is confirmed by the relative intensity of the diffraction peak for all TiO2-GQD combinations which is moderately less than that of the individual peak of bare TiO2. Moreover, there is no shift in the positions of the Bragg peaks when the concentration of GQD on TiO2 is increased which means GQD acts as a strain free layer, whereas there is increase in FWHM due to variation in the crystallite size as shown in Fig. 2b.

UV–Vis absorption spectra of pristine and TiO2 with different concentrations of GQDs are shown in Fig. 3 a. It is known that, when the size of particle decreases to that of Bohr radius the “size quantization” effect arises due to the spatial confinement of charge carriers thereby blue shift (R < aB) is observed in the absorption spectra (for TiO2 usually in the range of 1–12 nm). Moreover the “effective mass approximation model” describes a band gap variation with the particle size (2R) in both cases of weak (R > aB) and strong (R < aB) exciton confinement regimes [29,30]. Fig. 3a clearly reveals the strong absorption peaks are observed at ~318, 314, 306, 310 and 290 nm for pure, TG 5, TG 10, TG 20 and TG 30 respectively. The peaks of GQD modified samples are blue shifted with increase in the concentration of GQDs compared with the pristine TiO2 which is attributed to the variation of particle size. The calculated bandgap (through Tauc's plot) of the prepared samples are 2.62, 2.75, 2.91, 2.82 and 3.06 eV for pure, TG 5, TG 10, TG 20 and TG 30 respectively and the spectrum is displayed in Fig. 3b. It is known that the excitation dependent photoluminescence behaviour is usually observed in fluorescent carbon materials [31–35]. On changing the excitation wavelength from 310 nm to 500 nm, the emission wavelengths of GQDs moved to longer wavelength region and are shown in Fig. 3c. The PL spectrum of GQDs displays a strong peak of emission at 426 nm (π*- π transition) at an excitation wavelength of 310 nm with stokes shift of 96 nm. When the excitation wavelength is increased up to 500 nm the emission wavelength also increased correspondingly and it shifted from blue to green region where the emission intensity also gets affected correspondingly. Fig. 3d clearly depicts the variation of emission peak wavelength and the continual suppression of the blue emission with increasing excitation wavelength. This observed excitation dependent mechanism also is one of the confirmations of GQDs.

Fig. 4 a displays Raman spectra of bare TiO2 where Raman modes at 146.5 (Eg), 400 (B1g), 515.4 (A1g + B1g) and 638.7 (Eg) cm−1 were observed. Again the anatase phase of TiO2 nanoparticles is confirmed [36]. Similarly Fig. 4b shows the Raman spectrum of TiO2-GQD core shell nanostructure in which two broad peaks at around 1335 cm−1 and 1605 cm−1 are observed. These modes correspond to the breathing mode of k-point phonons of A1g representation and first order scattering of A2g phonons of sp2 carbon atoms, confirming the presence of GQD in the composite [37].

The morphology of the synthesized TiO2 and TiO2-GQDs are displayed in Fig. 5 . Fig. 5a–d shows the SEM and mapping spectrum of pristine TiO2. Likewise, Fig. 5e–j shows the SEM and mapping spectrum of TiO2-GQDs core shell nanostructures. SEM micrograph is clearly revealing GQDs were decorated on the surface of TiO2 for TG 10 when it compared with pristine TiO2. Moreover, it can be seen that the particles formed as spherical morphology with the strong agglomeration, and the mapping data revealed no impurities are present except Ti, O, C and N.

Fig. 6 a–b shows the TEM micrograph of pristine TiO2, where it is seen that the particles have spherical shape with the size ranging from 5 to 14 nm (Fig. 6c). Fig. 6d is the SEM image of prepared GO which looks like a sheet and it was broken in to small spherical sheets in alkaline atmosphere through hydrothermal treatment to form “Graphene Quantum Dots” as seen in Fig. 6e. Fig. 6g–h shows the TEM image of TG 10 nanostructure which clearly shows that GQDs are surrounded on the surface of TiO2. Though it is not clearly represented because of the comparable particle sizes of TiO2 and GQD. However the places marked on the image helps to understand the formation and the presence of GQDs on the surface of TiO2. Besides, for more clarification higher magnification images were taken which indicated that these nanoparticles have high degrees of crystallinity surrounded by GQDs which is represented in Fig. 6i–j. Further HRTEM analysis in Fig. 6i–j shows the lattice fringes with an interlayer distance of 0.35 nm lattice spacing of the (101) planes for anatase TiO2 and 0.21 nm for GQDs which is well consistent with XRD results. Moreover, elemental mapping analysis confirms the homogeneous distribution of Ti, O, C and N (Fig. 6k). The average particle size distribution spectrum of pristine TiO2, GQDs and TG 10 is displayed in Fig. 6c, f and l respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of HOMO and LUMO energy levels
ce:section.ce:para:
Cyclic voltammetry measurements were used to determine the LUMO and HOMO energy levels of the GQDs and hence to find the bandgap. Applying the potentials above the LUMO or below the HOMO can lead to abrupt development of cathodic and anodic currents respectively [38–40]. Electrode for electrochemical analysis was prepared by drop casting of GQDs in ethanol suspensions onto a graphite sheet electrode (area of 1 cm2) and dried at 40 °C. Fig. 7 a displays the behaviour of the graphite sheet in the absence and presence of the GQDs. During oxidation and reduction processes, one reversible reduction peak appeared at −0.76 V. The LUMO and HOMO positions of GQDs were calculated using the following equations [41]:
ELUMO = -(ERed+4.4 + 0.34) eV
EHOMO = -(ELUMO-Eg) eV
where ELUMO represents the LUMO position, ERed is the reduction peak value with respect to Ag/AgCl electrode, EHOMO represents the HOMO position and Eg is the optical band gap of the GQDs (Fig. 7c). The measured LUMO and HOMO positions are −3.98 eV and −7.04 eV respectively.

Nevertheless, this approach has proven to fail many times and it cannot be used to give a real and precise quantification of band positions. Because, the obtained band position from the CV analysis mainly depends on the electrode material and solvent. Hence in order to overcome this issue, Ultraviolet Photoelectron Spectra (UPS) has been taken (Fig. 7b) and the found value has been compared with the CV analysis. The HOMO level was determined from the onset of detected electrons (Fig. 7b) and the obtained value of GQDs is 6.18 eV which is in reasonable agreement with the reported value [42]. The conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) position of TiO2 and FTO electrode were obtained from the literature [43] and schematic representation of the charge transport mechanism in DSSC is shown in Fig. 7d.

To investigate the effect of GQDs in TiO2 photoanodes, the photovoltaic performance was studied as shown in Fig. 8 and the photovoltaic parameters obtained from the above J-V characteristics are given in Table 1 . Here the sample TG 10 cell shows a good increase in short circuit current density from 6.78 mA/cm2 to 9.34 mA/cm2 which is shown in Fig. 8a. Further increase in the concentration of GQDs is not useful as there is no improvement in the short circuit density. The observed results show an increase in conversion efficiency for an increase in the GQDs content in the photoanode and reaches a maximum for TG 10. Further increase in the concentration of GQDs showed only a decrease in the conversion efficiency. This may be due to more trapping sites instead of an electron pathway caused by the higher concentration of GQDs hindering the charge collection. The enhancement of efficiency of the GQDs based cells is due to the following factors: (i) Faster transport of photo-generated charge carriers from the dye LUMO level to conduction band of TiO2 due to assistance of GQDs (ii) Highly porous nature of TiO2 nanoparticles with the addition of GQDs which allow more dye molecules to get adsorbed leading to generation of more number of photo-electrons (iii) extended photo-absorption range (it acts as a sensitizer). The resulting PCE is quite low, due to the higher charge transfer resistance (Rct) at the interface of FTO/TiO2 and TiO2-GQDs/dye or TiO2-GQDs/electrolyte and voids in the photo anode. This problem can be overcome by incorporating a blocking layer or scattering layer on the photo-anode, as reported in literature. Hence here all the cells are treated with TTIP. After treating with TTIP, a drastic improvement was observed in both JSC and PCE. Among all the cells TTIP/TG10/TTIP cell showed a JSC of 13.77 mA/cm2 and the efficiency of 5.1%, which is 65% higher compared to the cell without TTIP treatment (3.09%) and it is shown in Fig. 8d. This treatment is also useful in increasing the particle interaction with TiO2-GQDs due to lattice matching. TiO2-GQDs nanoparticles could easily diffuse when coated on the scattering layer which would increase the connectivity of the particles and reduce the resistance at the interface. This would also reduce the voids in photo-anode and generate small crystallites on the surface which causes an increase in the dye adsorption and light harvesting capacity.

Also the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed to describe the internal electronic transfer process. EIS of DSSC where there are few interfaces, provides information on charge transport, transfer and accumulation processes in the cell such as the following:
(a)
charge transport due to electron diffusion through TiO2-GQD core shell structure
(b)
ionic diffusion in the electrolyte due to the above
(c)
charge transfer due to electron back reaction at the FTO/electrolyte interface and recombination at the photoanode/electrolyte interface
(d)
regeneration of the redox species at counter electrode/electrolyte interface and
(e)
charging of the TiO2-GQD capacitive element

Wang et al. [44] have explained the above based on a theoretical model. However, owing to the complexity of the system, the unambiguous assignment of equivalent circuits and the elucidation of processes occurring on dye-sensitized mesoscopic TiO2 electrode is difficult and remains still as a topic of research. There are many standard text books for the analysis of the above properties from EIS and so it is not explained in detail here. A simple understanding of these is possible from the work of Sarkar et al. [45] where a computer based spread sheet is given to elucidate the various properties. The EIS measurements were performed at the VOC under AM 1.5 illumination in the frequency range between 0.01 Hz and 100 KHz and the results are displayed in Fig. 8b and e. The semicircles in the middle frequency region for the DSSCs with GQDs modified photo-anodes were observed.

The radius of the semicircle of the device with the TiO2-GQDs layer is smaller than the pristine electrode, suggesting that the heterostructure of GQDs and TiO2 decreases the charge transfer resistance (Rct) as shown in Fig. 8b. The variation in Rct values depends on the concentration of the GQDs as it determines the charge injection process. Also an increase in the Rct value in TiO2-GQDs based photoanode was observed for the increasing GQDs content (TG-20, TG-30) which decreases the efficiency. Similar results were obtained for TTIP treated cells, where the charge transfer resistance is reduced by twofold when the scattering layer (TTIP) and blocking layer (TTIP) is deposited, compared to the untreated cells. Finally, among the fabricated cells, TTIP/TG10/TTIP cell exhibited a lower charge transfer resistance (Fig. 8e) and the measured values are displayed in Table 2 . Also the effect of GQDs and TTIP layer on electron lifetime are determined from Bode phase plots and are shown in Fig. 8c and f respectively. It is observed here that the frequency was shifted to the lower frequency range when GQDs are added which is also reflected in the J-V spectrum. The maximum frequencies (ωmax) in the middle frequency region of the Bode plots for pure and GQDs modified TiO2 (TG 10) were 613.13 Hz and 75.77 Hz respectively. But after treating with TTIP, the electron life times are significantly increased which reveals the suppression of electron recombinations. Since ωmax is inversely related to the electron lifetime, GQD modified TiO2 is a useful material for the fabrication of DSSC. The calculated values from the graph for Rct and life time are given in Table 2. The lower ωmax here for the treated core shell structure shows that it has longer lifetime and hence lower charge recombination in DSSC. Hence the origin of higher Jsc in TTIP/TG10/TTIP is understood from the Rct and charge transport dynamics. The results from the present investigation on TiO2-GQD core shell structured DSSC have thrown more light on the rich opportunities for this type of solar cell in future. In addition, Fig. 9 a–d exposes the importance of GQDs and TTIP on power, Jsc, efficiency respectively. Fig. 9c is clearly reveals the importance of GQDs and TTIP treatment on Jsc and among all TTIP/TG10/TTIP cell is optimized. Likewise, Fig. 9d plots the variation of efficiency with respect to the GQDs concentration. The repeatability test analysis carried out for TTIP treated cells and it reveals the deviation in JSC and η are of ± 7–13% which is derived from error bar diagram (Fig. 9e–f). Moreover, there are few reports available in literature for the solar cells based on graphene/GQDs composite with TiO2 with different synthesize technique and they are also presented in Table 3 , for comparison with the present results. Most of the work stated that graphene incorporation in the photo anode is one of the efficient ways to increasing the PCE. However, synthesis technique, formation and concentration are the major factors to deciding the PCE. In the present work, first time exposes the importance of GQDs on the core shell form and the obtained PCE is high compared to previously reported values.

For the first time solar cells are fabricated with TiO2-GQDs core/shell nanostructures and the effect of GQDs on the performance of the solar cells are studied. Basic characterizations were carried out to confirm the structure and other optical properties. Highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of the GQDs against vacuum are determined through cyclic voltammetry technique and UPS spectra. Among all the DSSCs, TG-10 based DSSC showed the optimal performance with a maximum Jsc of 9.34 mA cm−2 and efficiency of 3.09% for untreated and 13.77 mA cm−2 and efficiency of 5.10% for treated TTIP/TG10/TTIP cell which is higher than those of the pristine (untreated) based DSSC by 47% and 65% respectively. Impedance spectroscopy and Bode-phase plots suggested a decrease in the charge transfer resistance between FTO/TiO2/dye/electrolyte interface for the incorporation of GQDs and TTIP (TTIP/TG10/TTIP) and an increase in the light harvesting capacity and lifetime of the charge carriers apart from reduction in recombination as GQDs that acts as a good electron transporter.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The author I. John Peter thanks Department of Science and Technology, India for the financial support under DST-INSPIRE fellowship (Code: IF160974). Prof. K. Ramachandran acknowledges UGC Emeritus scheme, India for the support acknowledges UGC Emeritus scheme for the support. The authors thank CSIR-NEIST, Assam and CECRI, Karaikudi for providing the UPS and HRTEM measurements.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.solmat.2019.110239.
